questions.question: Under an SNT-TC-1A-style certification program, a Level I performing MT works directly to:
options: The text of the governing code
A part-specific written instruction or technique sheet
Verbal direction from the production supervisor
Personal judgment developed in training
questions.explanation: Level I personnel perform examinations to written instructions; the code's requirements reach them through the qualified procedure and its part-specific technique sheets.

questions.question: A 12-in.-long, 3-in.-diameter shaft is magnetized longitudinally in a 5-turn, low-fill-factor coil. Using the typical rule of thumb NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D), the coil current is about:
questions.explanation: L/D = 12/3 = 4, so NI ≈ 45,000/4 = 11,250 ampere-turns; divided by 5 turns that is about 2,250 A. The technique sheet value, verified with a QQI or gaussmeter, governs in practice.

questions.question: Welds on a structural steel building frame are most commonly examined under:
options: A structural welding code of the AWS D1.1 type
A tubular-goods purchaser specification of the API 5CT type
An aerospace standard practice of the ASTM E1444 type
A personnel qualification standard such as ISO 9712
questions.explanation: Structural steel work takes its examination and acceptance requirements from structural welding codes; tubular, aerospace, and personnel standards govern other domains.

questions.question: Which discontinuity originates at the inherent stage of the product's life?
options: Lap
Grinding crack
Blowhole
Fatigue crack
questions.explanation: Blowholes are gas pockets trapped during solidification of the ingot or casting — the inherent stage. Laps come from primary working, grinding cracks from secondary finishing, and fatigue from service.

questions.question: Compared with a surface crack, a lap typically produces an indication that is:
options: Bolder and more tightly held, because laps are wide open
Weak, irregular, and intermittent, because the fold meets the surface at a shallow angle with oxide trapped inside
Perfectly round and fuzzy
Visible only with the residual method
questions.explanation: A lap's shallow entry angle and trapped oxide leak far less flux than a crack standing square to the surface, so its indication is faint and broken — easy to miss without care.

questions.question: An overhauled shaft is prone to transverse fatigue cracks in its journals. Which magnetization must be included to find them?
options: A circular field from a head shot only
A longitudinal field from a coil or yoke, so flux crosses the transverse cracks at 45–90 degrees
Either field direction — fatigue cracks show in any field
No magnetization — transverse cracks are found visually
questions.explanation: A transverse crack lies across the shaft axis, so it needs flux running along the axis — longitudinal magnetization. A circular field would run parallel to the crack and barely leak.

questions.question: For an examination with visible (non-fluorescent) particles, the typical minimum lighting condition is:
options: At least 100 fc (about 1,000 lux) of white light at the examination surface
No more than 2 fc of white light at the surface
At least 1,000 µW/cm² of white light at the lamp
Whatever general shop lighting provides
questions.explanation: Visible-particle examinations typically require at least about 100 footcandles (roughly 1,000 lux) at the surface, verified with a calibrated light meter — the opposite end of the scale from the darkened fluorescent booth.

questions.question: After an operator doubles the amperage "to be sure," the part shows a heavy overall fur of particles with strong buildup at every edge. The correct reading is:
options: Multiple relevant indications — reject the part
False background from over-magnetization — return to the qualified amperage, re-verify the field, and re-run
Nonrelevant indications from section changes
Proof of widespread subsurface flaws
questions.explanation: Furring is excessive background caused by too much field, not leakage from discontinuities. The technique is corrected to the qualified value — typically proven with a QQI or a 30–60 G tangential-field check — and the examination is repeated.

questions.question: Which statement correctly separates interpretation from evaluation?
options: Interpretation compares the indication to acceptance criteria; evaluation decides its cause
Interpretation decides the cause (relevant, nonrelevant, false); evaluation compares a relevant indication's type, size, and location to the acceptance criteria for a disposition
They are two names for the same step
Evaluation happens before any indication is interpreted
questions.explanation: Interpretation names the cause of the indication; evaluation measures the relevant ones and applies the governing criteria to reach accept or reject.

questions.question: Criteria reject four or more rounded indications in a line when separated by 1/16 in. or less. You find four aligned rounded indications, each 1/32 in. apart. The disposition is:
options: Accept — each indication is individually small
Reject — the aligned-rounded condition is met, since 1/32 in. is less than 1/16 in.
Accept — alignment rules apply only to linear indications
Re-test with higher amperage to see if they merge
questions.explanation: The pattern matches the criterion exactly: four aligned rounded indications with spacing at or under the 1/16-in. threshold. Individual size does not rescue a rejected pattern — the criteria rule.
